rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the policy text itself (climate_screen=explicit_self): it proposes to "Deploy smart meters that provide real-time energy use information" to utility customers. Smart meters are a standard resource-efficiency/demand-management mechanism enabling reduced electricity consumption via feedback and load management, aligning with the existing canonical mechanism (smart_meter_deployment → energy_use_reduction). The gate failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') reflects missing explicit outcome wording, but the intervention is clearly energy-information/efficiency related and should remain in the climate dataset.
policy_statement: Deploy smart meters that provide real-time energy use information to all Seattle City Light customers (begin implementation by 2015).
mechanism_description: Begin implementing a plan to deploy smart meters that provide real-time energy-use information, helping customers identify opportunities to reduce electricity consumption and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same energy services → improved information/feedback → ↓ electricity demand → ↓ fuel combustion for power generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Utilities install smart meters and provide near-real-time usage data (often with apps/portals, alerts, and time-varying information) to influence customer behavior and enable more granular management of loads.  
(2) Mediating process: Better measurement and feedback reduces waste and shifts/curtails discretionary loads; in some cases it also enables demand response and peak reduction, lowering marginal generation needs.  
(3) Outcome: Lower overall electricity demand (and sometimes lower peak demand) reduces generation from fossil units and associated CO₂ (and co-pollutant) emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity consumption leading to lower power-generation GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: smart meter deployment → better information/control → reduced electricity use → reduced upstream emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The mechanism’s main lever is reducing energy input for the same service (efficiency via information), which fits Resource Efficiency; mitigation occurs downstream but is enabled by demand reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If deployed mainly to integrate renewables, reduce line losses, or improve outage response, classification could shift, but those are not the dominant effect described here.  
4) Applies to all instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is smart-meter-enabled customer/utility insight to reduce consumption follows the same “same service, less energy” pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence (e.g., whether paired with TOU pricing, demand response, or specific emissions targets).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “smart meter” program is implemented primarily for demand response/peak shifting without net energy reduction, the dominant effect could be grid optimization rather than energy-use reduction; if the policy explicitly targets emissions via marginal fossil displacement, it could be treated as Mitigation at the policy level, but the stated canonical mechanism implies demand/energy reduction.
key_indicators: “deploy smart meters”; “provide real-time energy use information”; “to all Seattle City Light customers”; “Begin implementing a plan”
co_benefits: Potential grid reliability/peak management improvements; improved billing accuracy/customer service; facilitation of demand response (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an information-disclosure/feedback program, but the policy statement’s central action is systemwide meter deployment (infrastructure rollout) rather than a standalone customer education/disclosure requirement.
